items.prompt_md: In the O(1)-min stack design, what does `min_stack[-1]` represent at any point in time?
options: The smallest value ever pushed, even if it has since been popped
The minimum of only the values currently on the data stack
The average of the top two elements
The index of the smallest element in the data stack
items.answer: The minimum of only the values currently on the data stack
items.explanation_md: Because `min_stack` is popped in lockstep with the data stack, its top always reflects the minimum of what's still there — not of everything that was ever pushed.

items.prompt_md: You need a stack supporting push, pop, and a `get_min()` call that must run in O(1) — not by rescanning the stack's contents. Which design fits?
options: Keep the stack's contents sorted at all times
Maintain a second, parallel stack that stores the running minimum alongside each pushed value
Recompute the minimum with a full scan every time `get_min` is called
Track values in a hash map keyed by value, and scan its keys for the smallest
items.answer: Maintain a second, parallel stack that stores the running minimum alongside each pushed value
items.explanation_md: A shadow stack that mirrors every push with "the minimum including this value" turns `get_min` into a single O(1) peek, and `pop` still works in O(1) because popping both stacks together restores the correct earlier minimum for free.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of `push`, `pop`, and `get_min` in the augmented min-stack design?
options: O(n) for get_min only, O(1) for push and pop
O(n) for push and pop, O(1) for get_min
items.explanation_md: Each operation only touches the top of one or both stacks — no scanning is ever needed, so push, pop, top, and get_min are all O(1), at the cost of O(n) extra space for the shadow stack.
